moveAnnotations.annotation: We're playing the Nimzo-Larsen Attack, an unconventional first move that prioritizes flexible piece placement over central occupation. By advancing the b-pawn, we prepare to develop our light-squared bishop to b2, where it will exert long-term pressure along the a1-h8 diagonal—especially aiming at f6 and e5. This move avoids the main theoretical battles of 1.e4 or 1.d4 while still supporting the center indirectly; we'll often follow up with moves like Nf3, e3, and Be2, building a solid setup. The Nimzo-Larsen appeals to players who prefer a patient, positional approach over sharp, forcing lines.

Black immediately strikes in the center with the Dutch Defense, claiming space on the kingside and preventing us from playing e4. The pawn on f5 controls e4 and g4, gives Black's light-squared bishop a future diagonal, and prepares to support a strong pawn center with moves like e6 or e5. This is an aggressive, double-edged response to our quiet opening—Black wants active counterplay and space, accepting some weakening of the e6 square and kingside light squares in exchange for dynamic piece activity. We'll need to continue developing patiently while preparing to meet Black's central ambitions.
